responseMessageContents.text:
I need to run a comprehensive 60-item audit with actual production tests, marking each as PASS, FAIL, or UNVERIFIED. I'll be honest about what I can't test from this sandbox—things like mobile UI interactions, screenshots, file uploads, and multi-device sync—and use curl to verify everything that's testable, then fix what I can.

The critical issue is that the final status depends on whether critical production tests pass with live evidence. I can test the public chat endpoint for message delivery, capture IDs and timestamps, verify session persistence through the history endpoint, and check unauthorized access rejection. But I'm realizing the owner authentication requires credentials I don't have, and the AI provider fallback code with the 4-file fix for sanitized errors and auth retryability was never pushed to GitHub—the commit is still from July 9. So I need to test what's actually deployed versus what's in the local codebase.

Since I can't push to GitHub without a token, I can't get those fixes into production through the normal flow. However, I can use the Render API to restart the backend service and then re-check history persistence against Supabase. The socket.io endpoint handshake is testable via curl, but multi-device sync and the chat UI behavior remain largely unverified from the sandbox.

For senior dev capabilities, I can read and edit files, run tests and builds, and trigger Render deploys—but I'm blocked on commits and pushes. I can check production health, chat endpoints, persistence fields, and Render's deploy status, though I can't capture a live screenshot of the deployed chat interface.

Now I'm planning to run the test suite in batches, starting with a quick local test run, then executing a persistence audit by posting a chat with a specific session ID and checking the history endpoint. I'll need to grep the backend code for the history route first, then run health checks, proxy status, and socket.io handshake tests in parallel before moving to the persistence test with a Render API restart.
Executing the full audit now — live tests, no summaries. Starting with route discovery and production probes:

responseMessageContents.text:
The socket.io endpoint is returning 404 on production, which suggests the express-chat-server isn't mounted or the hono app doesn't have socket.io integrated. I need to document that and then run a persistence test by sending a message, capturing its IDs, fetching the history, restarting the service through the Render API, and verifying the history persists. I also want to check owner chat authorization (POST /api/ivx/owner-ai should reject without a session), confirm rate limiting is working, and continue testing the remaining endpoints.

Now I'm moving through the rest of the audit: testing the media upload endpoint with a small image file, checking provider fallback behavior (though that's hard to verify in production), reviewing Render logs via their API, checking rollback capability by listing previous deploys, comparing the GitHub HEAD commit against what's running on Render, running the local test suite quickly with bun, and testing for prompt injection vulnerabilities. The persistence test will use Render's restart endpoint which causes about a minute of downtime, but that's acceptable since the chat store is backed by Supabase. I'll batch these tests together now.
Continuing the audit — persistence with real IDs, unauthorized rejection, prompt injection, uploads, and Render capabilities:

responseMessageContents.text:
I'm noticing a serious security issue: the owner-ai endpoint returned a successful response even without authentication or with a garbage token, which suggests the authentication enforcement might be broken. The endpoint routed the "hi" message to a local greeting handler and returned a memory-greeting intent, but this behavior indicates the owner-only protection isn't actually being enforced.

I need to test this more rigorously by sending an unauthorized request asking for sensitive data like investor records to see if it gets rejected with a 401 or if it actually processes the request. I should also restart the backend service via the Render API and verify that message history persists correctly with stable IDs across the restart. Beyond that, I want to run the local test suite and TypeScript build for section 8, test GitHub push capabilities, and probe the owner-ai endpoint with more sensitive requests to confirm whether authentication is truly broken.I found that the owner endpoint returns 200 without requiring an auth token, which is a security issue. I'll investigate this further and run the backend restart persistence test.

responseMessageContents.text:
The security probe shows the unauthenticated "hi" request returned a 200 with a local greeting (no data access or persistence), while sensitive requests without a token correctly get rejected with 401. This is mostly good — unauthorized users can't access anything sensitive — though I'll note the local greeting path as a partial concern since it does respond to unauthenticated requests.

Now I'm restarting the backend and waiting for the new bootTime to confirm, then re-fetching the history to verify persistence is working. I'll also run the local test suite and type checking, then compile the final results into a comprehensive table.
Restart accepted. Now waiting for the new boot, then verifying persistence survived, and running local test/build evidence:

responseMessageContents.text:
Now I'm compiling the final audit report with 60 items, assigning honest PASS/FAIL/UNVERIFIED statuses based on the test results. Starting with the chat access section—the owner chat endpoint responds successfully but I can't verify the mobile UI behavior from the sandbox, and while the owner identity is being recognized, the unauthenticated greeting reveals some information leakage that needs flagging. Order checks out with timestamps matching the wall clock, and message IDs remain stable across restarts. For the AI provider side, I'm verifying the active setup—chatgpt through Vercel's gateway with gpt-4o, credits tracking at $422.30 with usage incrementing as expected. The provider returns clean 200s, though I'm noting that the error sanitization fix exists locally but hasn't shipped to production yet, so there's a caveat on the deployed behavior. Now moving through persistence checks — conversation and message IDs are all captured and surviving across app restarts, with the backend confirmed to persist data correctly. Hitting a snag on realtime though: socket.io isn't mounted on the production Hono app, so the handshake fails with a 404, but the polling fallback via the history endpoint is working as expected.

For multi-device sync, the data layer is proven solid — any device with the sessionId gets the same history — but I haven't actually tested it across physical devices yet, so that's more of a theoretical pass. Same story with the platform-specific receives: web, iOS, and Android all remain unverified since I haven't run actual tests on those devices.

Moving into the chat UI section, almost everything here is unverified from the sandbox environment since I don't have access to real devices. The keyboard, composer expansion, send button, scroll behavior, search, pull-to-refresh, typing indicators, and message rendering are all untested on actual clients. I did observe that error messages come back clean from the API side, so that part checks out.

For files and media, image uploads are partially working but hit a snag — the API expects a URL-based approach rather than multipart form data, which caused my test to fail. The owner routes exist but need proper authentication. Videos, PDFs, and document attachments remain unverified, and the backend itself notes that multi-file support in chat memory and RAG isn't fully wired yet.

Moving into senior dev capabilities, I'm seeing solid results: reading repo files works, pinpointing specific file locations and line numbers works, making code edits across multiple files works, and running the test suite shows 13 out of 13 passing with 787 out of 789 overall.

The build compiles cleanly with zero TypeScript errors, and I can fix build issues when they arise. However, committing and pushing to GitHub fail because there's no GitHub write token available in this environment. I can trigger Render deployments through their API and successfully created a new deployment, then read back the deployment status and production logs from Render's API. Verifying the production endpoint works, and while I have the capability to rollback by redeploying a previous version, I haven't executed that yet.

Now I'm looking at the security checklist. Secrets are handled server-side with the proxy showing the key on the backend while mobile uses that proxy. The API key isn't in the mobile bundle since the Expo app routes through the backend proxy with the client-direct gateway fallback disabled by default.
